1: The visionary guide brings Ezekiel to the outer east gate of the sanctuary. This is the same east gate through which the glory of YHWH departed (10:19; 11:23) and returned (43:1-4). The gate's future status is now revealed — it will remain sealed permanently, its closing serving as a permanent memorial to the divine entry.
2: sagar yihyeh lo yippatach ('it shall be shut; it shall not be opened'): the absolute permanence of the closure established through doubled negative construction. Ve'ish lo-yavo vo ki YHWH Elohei Yisrael ba bo ('and no one shall enter by it, because YHWH the God of Israel entered by it'): the gate sealed by divine entry is thereby permanently reserved for YHWH. The logic is that of holiness by contact — the gateway touched by the divine presence becomes permanently sacred and off-limits to ordinary use.
3: hanasi hanasi hu yeshev-bo ('the prince, the prince — he shall sit in it'): the emphatic double title. Nasi (prince) — Ezekiel's deliberate choice throughout chapters 40-48 to avoid melek (king). The kings had profaned YHWH's sanctuary (43:7-9); in the restored order the human leader has a different title and a different relationship to the sacred space. The prince may enter by the vestibule and eat a covenantal meal in the gateway — a unique privilege marking his mediating role.
4: vayavi'eni derekh sha'ar hatzafon el-pnei habayit ('and he brought me by the way of the north gate to the front of the house'): Ezekiel is repositioned for a new view. va'ereh vehinneh male kavod-YHWH et-beit YHWH ('I looked and behold, the glory of YHWH filled the house of YHWH'): the kavod (glory) that departed in chapters 10-11 is now fully resident in the restored temple. Ezekiel's face-down response mirrors his response to the initial glory vision (1:28; 3:23).
5: sim libecha ('set your heart/mind'): sim lev (to direct attention, take note) — the same idiom used in 40:4 at the beginning of the temple tour. YHWH instructs Ezekiel to pay careful attention to all the ordinances and laws of the house — both the exits and entrances (kol-motza'ei hammiqdash) and all the regulations (kol-Torah hammiqdash). The emphasis on careful observation prepares for the detailed priestly legislation that follows.
6: ve'amarta el-meri el-beit Yisrael ('and say to the rebellious house of Israel'): the oracle is directed to the rebellious house — the term meri (rebellious) was Ezekiel's epithet for Israel from the beginning (2:3, 5-8; 3:26-27). Even in the context of the restoration vision, the audience is reminded of the identity that made the departure of the glory necessary. The restoration is not earned but gracious.
7: behavi'achem bnei-neichar arelei-lev va'aralei basar ('when you brought foreigners, uncircumcised of heart and uncircumcised of flesh'): the double uncircumcision — of heart and body. Ben-neichar (foreigner) — specifically a foreign national, not the ger (resident alien) who could be incorporated into the covenant. Aral-lev (uncircumcised of heart): the spiritual-moral category; aral-basar (uncircumcised of flesh): the physical-covenant mark. Deuteronomy 10:16 calls for heart-circumcision alongside physical circumcision; Ezekiel requires both for sanctuary access.
8: velo shemartem mishmeret qodoshai ('and you have not kept the charge of my holy things'): shameret (charge/keeping/guard-duty) — the specific custodial responsibility of sanctuary service. Israel appointed others to perform the duties they should have done themselves. The specific failure named: outsiders in the sanctuary. The result is the profaning of YHWH's covenant — lehafar et-briti.
9: koh amar Adonai YHWH kol-ben-neichar aral-lev va'aral-basar lo-yavo el-miqdashi ('thus says the Lord YHWH: no foreigner, uncircumcised of heart and uncircumcised of flesh, shall enter my sanctuary'): the absolute exclusion rule for the new temple. The doubling of the condition (heart AND flesh) distinguishes the foreigner from those who might have physical circumcision without covenantal allegiance, or covenantal allegiance without the covenant sign.
10: ki im-haleviyim asher-rachaqu me'alai ('but the Levites who went far from me'): the Levites who participated in Israel's apostasy — who served at the gillulim (idols) when Israel went astray — receive a demoted status in the restored order. Venasa'u avontam ('they will bear their iniquity'): their punishment is the loss of priestly status. They retain temple service but not altar ministry — a permanent consequence of their historical infidelity.
11: meshartim babayit ('serving in the house'): the Levites' reduced role — gatekeepers (she'arim hasha'ar), temple attendants (meshartim et-habayit), slaughtering the burnt offering and sacrifice for the people. They serve the people but not directly at the altar of YHWH. Hem ya'amdu lifneihem leshartam ('they shall stand before them to serve them'): the preposition lifneihem (before them = the people) rather than lifnei YHWH (before YHWH) marks the demotion.
12: ya'an asher sher'tu otam lifnei gilluleihem ('because they served them before their idols'): the gillulim again — the contemptuous dung-word for idols. The Levites ministered to Israel in the idolatrous context, facilitating rather than opposing the apostasy. Vehayah leVet Yisrael lemikshol avon ('and became a stumbling block of iniquity to the house of Israel'): the Levites who were supposed to prevent stumbling became the stumbling block.
13: velo yigshu elai lechahanen li ('and they shall not draw near to me to minister as priests to me'): the nagash (draw near) vocabulary of priestly approach is denied to the apostate Levites. Likol-qodshai ('to any of my holy things'): exclusion from the entire realm of direct sacred ministry. They retain some temple function but not the constitutive function — the approach to YHWH in priestly mediation. Venasu klimatam uveto'avotam asher asu ('they shall bear their shame and their abominations which they committed').
14: unatati otam shomrei mishmeret habayit ('and I will make them keepers of the charge of the house'): the specific role assigned to the demoted Levites — custodial maintenance. Lekhol-avodat ('for all the service'): the full range of support and maintenance tasks, but not the altar service. The Levitical demotion is not expulsion but reassignment — they remain in the covenant community but with different responsibilities that reflect the permanent mark of their historical infidelity.
15: uhakohanim haleviyim bnei Tzadok ('but the priests, the Levites, the sons of Zadok'): the contrast clause — asher shamru et-mishmeret miqdashi ('who kept the charge of my sanctuary'). The sons of Zadok maintained their priestly faithfulness during Israel's apostasy. Hem yiqrevu elai lesharteni ('they shall draw near to me to serve me'): the same nagash (draw near) that was denied to the apostate Levites is now granted to the faithful line. Access to the divine presence is the mark and reward of fidelity.
16: hem yavo'u el-miqdashi ('they shall enter my sanctuary'): the sons of Zadok as the full priests of the restored temple. Ve'hemah yiqrevu el-shulchani lesharteni ('and they shall approach my table to serve me'): the 'table of YHWH' — the altar as dining table. The metaphor of divine hospitality expressed in the altar-table imagery appears in Ezekiel 41:22 and 44:16; YHWH as host, the priests as servants of the table, Israel as the guests. Veshemru et-mishmarti ('they shall keep my charge').
17: uvevo'am el-sha'are hechatzer hapnimit bigdei pishtim yilbashu ('when they enter the gates of the inner court, linen garments they shall wear'): pishtim (linen/flax) — the priestly material prescribed since Exodus 28:39-42. Wool is explicitly forbidden in v.17b: lo ya'aleh alehem tzemer ('wool shall not come up on them while serving'). The reason given in v.18 — 'so that they do not sweat' — reflects the purity concern about bodily secretion in the sacred space.
18: pea'rei pishtim yihyu al-rosham ('linen headcoverings shall be on their heads'): pe'er (turban/headcovering) — the priestly head covering. Umiknesei-pishtim yihyu al-motneihem ('linen breeches/undergarments shall be on their waists'): complete linen from head to waist. Lo yachgeru bayya' ('they shall not gird themselves with anything causing sweat'): the bodily integrity requirement — the priestly body in sacred service must not produce the secretion associated with labor and physical exertion.
19: ubetzetam el-hechatzer hachitzonah ('when they go out to the outer court'): the transition from holy to common space requires a change of garments. Ufishtenu bigdeihem asher-yeshartam bam ('they shall put off the linen garments in which they have been serving'): two sets of garments — one for the inner court (holy), one for the outer court (common). Levelo-yequaddesh et-ha'am bevigdeihem ('lest they consecrate/sanctify the people with their garments'): holiness is contagious — the sacred garments can transfer sanctity to ordinary people who contact them, requiring a buffer between the holy and common realms.
20: verosham lo yegale'chu ('and their heads they shall not shave'): the prohibition on priestly shaving of the head (cf. Lev 21:5). Vefara lo yeshallachu ('and they shall not let their hair grow wild/loose'): the priest must not adopt the unkempt hair of the mourner or the Nazirite-like long growth. Kasom yiksemu et-ra'sheihem ('trimming they shall trim their heads'): the middle path — neither shaved nor uncontrolled, regularly trimmed. The priest's body itself is regulated as part of sacred service.
21: veyayin lo yishteh kol-kohen bevo'am el-hechatzer hapnimit ('and no priest shall drink wine when they enter the inner court'): the alcohol prohibition in sacred service from Leviticus 10:9 (where the context is Nadab and Abihu's disastrous unauthorized fire — perhaps connected to intoxication). The inner court is the highest zone of sacred proximity; full cognitive clarity is required for service there.
22: ve'almana ugershusha lo-yiqchu lahem lenashim ('and a widow or a divorcée they shall not take as wives for themselves'): the stricter priestly marriage code. Ki im-betulot mizera beit Yisrael ('but only virgins from the seed of the house of Israel'): and widows of priests (ve'ha'almana asher tihyeh almana mikohen yiqachu). The high priest's restriction from Leviticus 21:14 is applied more broadly to all priests in the new temple order — the priestly family's purity reflecting the sanctuary's higher holiness.
23: ve'et-ami yoruu bein qodesh lechol ('and my people they shall teach between holy and common'): the core priestly teaching function — havdalah (distinction, separation), the ability to distinguish and communicate the difference between sacred and ordinary. Uvein-tame utahor yori'um ('and between clean and unclean they shall teach them'): the full range of purity distinctions that structure Levitical religion. The priests as teachers of the sacred/common and clean/unclean distinctions is the foundational purpose of the priestly class (cf. Lev 10:10-11; Deut 33:10; Mal 2:7).
24: val-riv hemah ya'amdu lishpot ('and in a dispute they shall stand to judge'): the priests as civil judges in cases of dispute. Bemishpatai yishpetahu ('according to my ordinances they shall judge'): YHWH's Torah as the legal code for the priestly court. Ve'et-torotai vechukotai bechol-mo'adai yishmoru ('and my instruction and statutes at all my festivals they shall observe'): the priestly role of legal, liturgical, and educational mediation fully described.
25: ve'el-met adam lo yavo litame' ('and for a dead person he shall not come to be defiled'): corpse impurity — the most severe level of uncleanness in Levitical law (Num 19). Only for the closest relatives — father, mother, son, daughter, brother, or unmarried sister — may a priest contract corpse impurity. Ve'im-lev lav yitamma''u ('but for these they may defile themselves'): the family exception to the general priestly prohibition on corpse contact.
26: ve'achar taharo shiv'at yamim yisperu-lo ('and after his purification, seven days they shall count for him'): the seven-day purification period after corpse defilement (Num 19:11-14). The counting begins the purification process — time as the healing mechanism within the purity system. Veyom tiharto ('on the day of his cleansing'): a fixed point after which priestly service can resume.
27: uveyom bo'o el-haQodesh ('and on the day he enters the holy place'): the reintegration offering. El-hechatzer hapnimit lesharet baQodesh ('to the inner court to minister in the holy place'): on the day the purified priest returns to full duty, he must offer a chatat (sin offering). The purification process is not merely the passage of time but requires a priestly sacrifice to restore full sacred access.
28: uhayetah lahem lenachala ani nachalatam ('and it shall be their inheritance — I am their inheritance'): the most theologically charged verse in the priestly legislation. Ani nachalatam ('I am their inheritance') echoes Numbers 18:20 ('I am your portion and your inheritance among the Israelites') and the Levitical principle from Joshua 13:14 that YHWH himself is the Levitical inheritance. Ve'achuzah lo-titenu lahem beYisrael ('and possession you shall not give them among Israel'): the priests receive no land — YHWH is their land.
29: hamincha vehacha'tat veha'asham hemah yocheluhu ('the grain offering, the sin offering, and the guilt offering — they shall eat them'): the priestly portion from the sacrificial system. The three offerings eaten by priests at the sanctuary represent YHWH's provision for their physical needs. Vechol-cherem beYisrael lahem yihyeh ('and every dedicated/devoted thing in Israel shall belong to them'): cherem (dedicated thing) — items vowed or devoted to YHWH become priestly property.
30: vere'shit kol-bikkurei kol ukhol-terumah mikol ('and the best of all the first-fruits of all, and every offering of all'): the first-fruits (bikkurim) and contributions (terumah) belong to the priests. Re'shit arissoteichem titenu lakohen ('the first portion of your dough you shall give to the priest'): even the dough-offering (arisah = dough, perhaps the first bread from the millstone) is priestly. Lehaniach berakhah el-beitecha ('to bring blessing upon your house'): the connection between giving first-fruits and receiving blessing — the economic order of worship sustains both priests and worshippers.
31: kol-nevela uterefah min-ha'of umin-habehemah lo yochlu hakohanim ('all carcass or torn animal — whether bird or beast — the priests shall not eat'): the standard Levitical prohibition on eating animals that die naturally or are killed by predators (Lev 22:8). The priests must not eat from death; their food must come through proper slaughter. This closes the priestly code of chapter 44 — the priest's complete separation from the unclean, the common, and death itself, sustained by YHWH's own provision as their inheritance.
